VLP Battery — Electricity Company Model

Per-customer unit economics for a behind-the-meter battery operated by a licensed electricity supplier. Battery trades on wholesale + ancillary markets. Consumer demand served from grid, offset by battery discharge.

Adjust Inputs Charts are static — tables & break-even update live
Battery revenue includes arbitrage (MILP-optimised), frequency response, capacity market, balancing mechanism, and avoided network charges on kWh served from battery. Higher consumption = more avoided charges. Consumption levels modelled: 4,000 kWh, 5,000 kWh, 6,000 kWh.

Battery Revenue Breakdown

Trading revenue (arb + FR + CM + BM) is the same for all consumption levels — the battery trades on the full 15.36 kWh / 6 kW. But avoided network charges vary: kWh served to the consumer from the battery don't incur TNUoS/DUoS/BSUoS (5.25p/kWh), so higher consumption = more consumer-served discharge = more avoided charges.

ConsumptionArb £FR £CM £BM £ Consumer Served kWhAvoided Network £Total Battery Rev £
4,000 kWh/yr 306 43 50 2 735 39 439
5,000 kWh/yr 306 43 50 2 919 48 448
6,000 kWh/yr 306 43 50 2 1,102 58 458

Key Parameters

Battery Hardware

ParameterValueNotes
Capacity15.36 kWhUsable LFP cells
Inverter power6 kWMax charge/discharge rate
Duration2.56 hrsCapacity / power
Round-trip efficiency90%One-way: 94.87%
SoC operating range10% – 90%Usable: 12.29 kWh (80% of total)
Daily SoC start/end10% (1.54 kWh)Allows full charge-discharge cycle per day
Grid draw per full charge16.19 kWh= capacity / one-way efficiency
Energy delivered per discharge14.57 kWh= capacity x one-way efficiency
Charge time (full)2.70 hrsInverter-constrained
Discharge time (full)2.43 hrsInverter-constrained

Battery Cost & Financing

ParameterValueNotes
Total cost (hardware + install)£2,800Overrides CSV default of £2,600
Economic life10 yearsStraight-line depreciation
Annual depreciation£280/yrP&L only — excluded from cashflow
Loan interest rate7% p.a.On declining balance, equal principal repayments
Corporation tax25%Applied to EBT each year, no loss carry-forward
Loan periods modelled3-yr, 5-yr, 7-yr, 10-yr
Model horizon12 years10-yr loan gets 2 post-payoff years
Annual degradation2%/yrCompound; yr5 retention 92.2%, yr10 83.4%, yr12 80.1%

Grid Services Revenue

ServiceRateAnnual (full battery)Notes
Frequency Response (FR)£34/kW/yr£204 maxMILP jointly optimises FR vs arb hour-by-hour; actual FR < max
Capacity Market (CM)£40/kW/yr£50De-rated 20.9% (2-hr battery class, 2.6h duration)
Balancing Mechanism (BM)10% win x 5% uplift on arbVariesEstimated as % of arbitrage, not explicitly dispatched
Avoided network charges5.25p/kWh served from batteryVaries by consumptionTNUoS 0.87p + DUoS 3.63p + BSUoS 0.75p

Avoided network charges on battery-served kWh (TNUoS + DUoS + BSUoS = 5.25p/kWh) are subtracted from opex.

Spread Compression Scenarios

Scenarioalpha (BESS compression)beta (renewable widening)Yr 1 scaleYr 6 scaleYr 12 scale
Best0.460.41.0000.8770.880
Base0.50.31.0000.8150.777
Worst0.650.01.0000.6540.549

Formula: Spread = Floor + (S0 - Floor) x (C0/C)^alpha x (R/R0)^beta
Floor: 20.0 GBP/MWh | 2025 baseline spread: 73.0 GBP/MWh | Capacity data: data/inputs/capacity_forecasts.csv
Battery revenue (arb + FR + BM) in years 2+ is scaled by each year's factor. CM and avoided network are unscaled.

Assumptions & Limitations

1. Perfect foresight on prices. The MILP optimises each day's charge/discharge schedule using the full day's actual prices. A real dispatch algorithm would achieve a lower spread, potentially 10-30% below this estimate.
2. Battery degradation modelled at 2%/yr. Capacity declines 2% per year (compound). By year 10, retention is 81.7%. All battery revenue (arbitrage, FR, CM, BM, avoided network charges) is scaled by the degradation factor each year, in addition to spread compression.
3. Static customer tariff. Customer revenue is fixed at the modelled p/kWh + monthly fee for the entire period. In practice tariffs are reviewed annually against changing wholesale prices, network charges, and competitor rates.
4. Grid services assumed contractually secured. FR and CM revenues are modelled as certain annual income. In practice both require competitive tender wins. CM payments are subject to interconnector and demand-side response competition. FR rate (£34.0/kW/yr) assumes Dynamic Containment or equivalent; actual DC rates have fallen to £1-5/MW/h in recent clearing.
5. BM estimated, not modelled. Balancing Mechanism revenue is a simple 10% x 5% uplift on arbitrage net, not an explicit gate-closure dispatch model. Actual BM participation is volume-based and highly variable.
6. Fixed equal-principal loan repayment. The model uses equal annual principal instalments (not annuity/amortising). Real facilities typically use equal total payments, giving higher early payments and lower later ones — slightly different cashflow profiles.
7. Interest rate held constant at 7%. Reasonable for a fixed-rate facility but does not capture floating-rate risk or refinancing at maturity.
8. Corporation tax simplified. Tax at 25% on standalone EBT each year. Group relief, prior-year losses, R&D credits, Full Expensing capital allowances, and ring-fencing are all excluded. Effective tax rate likely lower in early years due to capital allowances.
9. No working capital. The cashflow model omits receivables, payables, and cash reserves. A real business would require operating capital, increasing the effective funding need above the battery cost alone.
10. Single customer, single battery. All results are per-customer. Portfolio effects — load diversification, operational leverage, shared grid service contracts — are not captured and would improve unit economics at scale.
11. EUR/GBP fixed at 0.85. GBP appreciation would reduce both supply cost and arbitrage revenue; depreciation would increase both. Net effect uncertain depending on pass-through arrangements.
12. UK use profile applied. Hourly consumption shape from use_profiles.csv (UK). Scaled proportionally to each consumption level. Different customer types (heat pumps, EVs) would have different peak patterns affecting avoided network charges.
13. Spread compression is market-level only. The compression formula models how aggregate BESS build-out reduces wholesale spreads. It does not capture: individual battery positioning, intraday price volatility changes, or potential new revenue streams (e.g. local flexibility markets, EV smart charging coordination).
14. MILP allows multiple intraday cycles. The optimiser can charge-discharge more than once per day if profitable (avg ~1.7 cycles/day observed). This is physically possible but increases wear; real dispatch may be more conservative to preserve battery life.
15. CM stress events assumed rare. Capacity Market revenue assumes the battery is usually available for other activities. If CM delivery obligations conflict with arbitrage/FR, actual revenue would be lower.
